# Flow Cytometry Activation Assay

## Current Best Practice

For cytokine-suppression questions, the current source suggests separating early activation markers from later effector markers in the same assay. CD69 alone would have missed the main IL-42 effect, while granzyme B and IFN-gamma captured the functional suppression more clearly.

## Key Findings

- [supports] A 24-hour flow-cytometry assay measuring CD69, IFN-gamma, and granzyme B distinguished preserved activation entry from reduced effector output. [[lee-2026-il42-cd8-suppression]]
- [extends] Granzyme B was the most informative readout for the IL-42 effect and should be prioritised when testing similar suppression hypotheses. [[lee-2026-il42-cd8-suppression]]
- [extends] Pairing the assay with a transwell condition helped test whether the suppressive effect required direct contact. [[lee-2026-il42-cd8-suppression]]

## Common Pitfalls

- Over-interpreting CD69 as a complete proxy for effector function.
- Using only cell-line models when primary-cell magnitude may differ.
- Drawing mechanistic conclusions from a single time point.

## Open Questions

- What is the best time-course design for separating activation entry from effector decline?
- Which additional cytotoxic markers should be added alongside granzyme B?
